coredata.dc:title: Highly permeable thin film composite hollow fiber membranes for brackish water desalination by incorporating amino functionalized carbon quantum dots and hypochlorite treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Gai, Wenxiao
Zhang, Yu
Zhao, Qipeng
Chung, Tai-Shung
coredata.dc:description: A novel strategy has been demonstrated to synergistically improve the separation performance of thin film composite (TFC) hollow fiber membranes for brackish water reverse osmosis (BWRO) desalination. It consists of two steps. Firstly, a series of amino functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized and incorporated into the polyamide layer of TFC membranes. The modified TFC membrane with the optimal amino functionalized CQDs has a pure water permeability (PWP) of 5.50 LMH/bar and a NaCl rejection of 98.0% against a 2000 ppm NaCl feed solution at 15 bar. Comparing with the unmodified TFC membrane, the modified TFC membrane has a 42.1% higher PWP without compromising the salt rejection. Then the modified TFC membrane was treated by a sodium hypochlorite solution at different concentrations. Chlorine was bonded to the polyamide layers, enhancing their repulsion against charged solutes and making the polyamide network less cross-linked. As a result, both PWP and NaCl rejection of the TFC membranes could be improved. After being treated with a sodium hypochlorite aqueous solution at 4000 ppm for 3h, the aforementioned modified TFC membrane displays a PWP of 10.13 LMH/bar and a NaCl rejection of 98.9% against a 2000 ppm NaCl solution at 15 bar, which demonstrates a great potential for brackish water desalination. This study may mark a milestone for the development of future BWRO membranes based on the use of amino functionalized CQDs and hypochlorite treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Reverse osmosis (RO)
Amino functionalized carbon quantum dots
Hypochlorite treatment
Nanomaterials
Interfacial polymerization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Syntheses and characterizations of the amino functionalized CQDs
Fabrication of the hollow fiber substrates of TFC membranes
Fabrication, modification and post-treatment of the polyamide layers of TFC membranes
Characterizations of the polyamide layers of TFC membranes
Separation performance of the TFC membranes
Characterizations of the polyamide layers incorporated with CQDs
Separation performance of the TFC membranes incorporated with CQDs
Characterizations of the polyamide layer of TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 after hypochlorite treatment
Separation performance of TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 after hypochlorite treatment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of the amino functionalized CQDs in comparison with the control.
FESEM images of (a) cross-section and (b) surface of the polyamide layers of (1) unmodified TFC membrane, (2) TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0)-1, (3) TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 and (4) TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.2)-1.
AFM results of the polyamide layers of (a) the unmodified TFC membrane, (b) TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0)-1, (c) TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 and (d) TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.2)-1.

(a) Pure water permeability (Feed: DI water) and (b) salt rejection (Feed: 2000 ppm NaCl aqueous solution) of the unmodified TFC membrane, TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0)-1, TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 and TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.2)-1. (Measured at 15 bar after the membranes had been stabilized at 20 bar for 1h.)

FESEM images of (a) cross-section and (b) surface of the polyamide layer of TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 (1) without any treatment or treated with a sodium hypochlorite aqueous solution at (2) 2000 ppm, (3) 4000 ppm and (4) 8000 ppm for 3h.
AFM results of the polyamide layer of TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 (a) without any treatment or treated with a sodium hypochlorite aqueous solution at (b) 2000 ppm, (c) 4000 ppm or (d) 8000 ppm for 3h.

(a) Pure water permeability (Feed: DI water) and (b) salt rejection (Feed: 2000 ppm NaCl aqueous solution) of TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 without any treatment or treated with a sodium hypochlorite aqueous solution at 2000 ppm, 4000 ppm or 8000 ppm for 3h (Measured at 15 bar after the membranes had been stabilized at 20 bar for 1h).
XPS results (mass ratio, %) of the amino functionalized CQDs in comparison with theta control.
XPS results (mass ratio, %) of the polyamide layers of the unmodified TFC membrane, TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0)-1, TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 and TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.2)-1.
XPS results (mass ratio, %) of the polyamide layer of TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 without any treatment or treated with a sodium hypochlorite aqueous solution at 2000 ppm, 4000 ppm or 8000 ppm for 3h.
Comparison with selected state-of-the-art BWRO membranes incorporated with nanomaterials reported in the literature.

head.ce:title: Highly permeable thin film composite hollow fiber membranes for brackish water desalination by incorporating amino functionalized carbon quantum dots and hypochlorite treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, National University of Singapore, 4 Engineering Drive 4, Singapore, 117585, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, National University of Singapore, 4 Engineering Drive 4, Singapore, 117585, Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel strategy has been demonstrated to synergistically improve the separation performance of thin film composite (TFC) hollow fiber membranes for brackish water reverse osmosis (BWRO) desalination. It consists of two steps. Firstly, a series of amino functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized and incorporated into the polyamide layer of TFC membranes. The modified TFC membrane with the optimal amino functionalized CQDs has a pure water permeability (PWP) of 5.50 LMH/bar and a NaCl rejection of 98.0% against a 2000 ppm NaCl feed solution at 15 bar. Comparing with the unmodified TFC membrane, the modified TFC membrane has a 42.1% higher PWP without compromising the salt rejection. Then the modified TFC membrane was treated by a sodium hypochlorite solution at different concentrations. Chlorine was bonded to the polyamide layers, enhancing their repulsion against charged solutes and making the polyamide network less cross-linked. As a result, both PWP and NaCl rejection of the TFC membranes could be improved. After being treated with a sodium hypochlorite aqueous solution at 4000 ppm for 3h, the aforementioned modified TFC membrane displays a PWP of 10.13 LMH/bar and a NaCl rejection of 98.9% against a 2000 ppm NaCl solution at 15 bar, which demonstrates a great potential for brackish water desalination. This study may mark a milestone for the development of future BWRO membranes based on the use of amino functionalized CQDs and hypochlorite treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: The access of clean or potable water has become a serious issue afflicting people all over the world due to the rapid climate change, population growth and industrialization [1]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop efficient water treatment technologies to meet the drastic increase in clean water demand. The membrane-based separation technology, such as nanofiltration (NF) and reverse osmosis (RO), has been widely recognized as an effective water treatment approach to produce clean or potable water from various water sources [2–4]. Through these processes, clean water could be produced by filtering the contaminated water through a semi-permeable membrane under pressures. Among them, RO is the most predominant technology worldwide for brackish water and seawater desalination owing to its simplicity, excellent performance and low energy consumption [5,6].

Currently, the most prevalent RO membranes in the industry are thin film composite (TFC) membranes, which are manufactured by depositing a thin polyamide layer through interfacial polymerization of m-phenylene diamine (MPD) and 1, 3, 5-benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC) on top of an ultrafiltration membrane [7,8]. TFC membranes are featured by superior permeation properties, monovalent salt rejection, and excellent stability in alkaline and acidic environments [5]. In addition, both support and selective layers of the TFC membranes could be tailored individually to satisfy the unique requirements of different applications [9–11]. However, there still exist some limitations and challenges impeding the performance of TFC membranes to meet the tightening requirements of water quality and energy efficiency [5]. One must overcome the trade-off relationship observed between water permeability and solute selectivity to further improve the TFC membranes [12,13]. Up to date, various physicochemical approaches have been proposed to modify the polyamide layer of TFC membranes to improve its separation performance, such as (1) changing the interfacial polymerization reaction using additives, (2) post-treating the polyamide selective layer with chloride, alkaline, alcohol, etc. and (3) anchoring a hydrophilic layer onto the polyamide layer via coating and grafting [14–19].

Lately, nanomaterials have brought some breakthroughs to the membrane-based separation technology because of their unique and outstanding characteristics, such as high hydrophilicity, molecular-sieving selectivity and chemical resistance [5,20]. Jeong et al. firstly incorporated NaA zeolite nanoparticles into the polyamide layer, the resultant TFC membrane had a pure water permeability (PWP) double of the control one [21]. Since then, a variety of nanomaterials have been utilized to modify the polyamide layers of TFC membranes, such as silica, inorganic salts, graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), etc. [22–28]. It has been reported that the incorporated nanomaterials could effectively modify the physicochemical properties of the polyamide layers, resulting in substantially enhanced separation performance [29]. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been of great interest recently because of their unique nanometer size [29–32]. In addition, the CQDs are featured with superior physicochemical properties, such as high hydrophilicity, large specific surface area, rich surface chemistry for functionalization, etc. [33,34].

To overcome the trade-off relationship observed between water permeability and solute selectivity, one could impart the unique physicochemical properties of CQDs into the polyamide layer via covalent bonds between CQDs and the polyamide chains. Since the amino groups of MPD react with TMC during the interfacial polymerization to form the cross-linked polyamide, CQDs with amino functionality may be able to participate with the cross-linking reaction during the polymerization. This would result in a novel polyamide layer with improved physicochemical properties and separation performance. Therefore, in the first portion of this paper, we attempted to synthesize various amino functionalized CQDs and incorporated them into the polyamide layer of TFC hollow fiber membranes to explore their application for brackish water desalination. Besides, it is known that the polyamide layer of TFC membranes is susceptible to the exposure of active chlorine species. Some studies showed that the exposure led to a lower water flux with an increased or decreased salt rejection [35–38], while others reported that the hypochlorite treatment improved water flux and salt rejection [39–41]. This controversy inspired us to examine the effects of hypochlorite treatment on our newly developed TFC membranes incorporated with amino functionalized CQDs in the second portion of this study. Therefore, a systematic investigation was conducted to define its effects and explore if one can further increase PWP without compromising salt rejection by the hypochlorite treatment.

Citric acid (CA, 99.5%, Sigma-Aldrich), ethanolamine (EA, 99.5%, Sigma-Aldrich) and sodium hydroxide (98%, Sigma-Aldrich) were used to synthesize the amino functionalized CQDs. The hollow fiber substrates of the TFC membranes were fabricated from a spinning dope made of Radel® A polyethersulfone (PES, Solvay Advanced Polymer), N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, 99.5%, Merck), polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG, Mw = 400g/mol, Acros Organics) and deionized (DI) water (Millipore, USA). The as-spun hollow fiber substrates were post-treated with a mixture of DI water and glycerol (Industrial grade, Aik Moh Paints & Chemicals Pte. Ltd.) before the interfacial polymerization. The polyamide layers of the TFC membranes were prepared via the interfacial polymerization reaction between 1, 3, 5-benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC, 98%, Sigma-Aldrich) dissolved in hexane (99.9%, Fisher Chemicals) and m-Phenylenediamine (MPD, 98%, Tokyo Chemical Industry) dissolved in DI water with the aid of sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, 99%, Sigma-Aldrich) as the surfactant. A sodium hypochlorite solution (NaClO, 4.00–4.99%, Sigma-Aldrich) was used to treat the polyamide layers. Sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5%, Merck) and DI water were used for the tests of membrane separation performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Syntheses and characterizations of the amino functionalized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize the amino functionalized CQDs, mixtures of ethanolamine (EA) and grinded citric acid (CA) at different ratios (i.e., 0:10, 1:10 and 1:5) were heated at 180°C in air for 3h in a glass beaker covered with a glass slide. To be more specific, 0g, 3.5g or 7g ethanolamine was added into 35g grinded citric acid, respectively. Subsequently, the reacted mixtures were dissolved in DI water forming aqueous solutions after they had been cooled down to room temperature, then the solutions were dialyzed using Slide-A-Lyzer G2 Dialysis Cassettes (2K MWCO) against DI water to remove the remaining molecular precursors. After the dialysis, the residues were neutralized with a sodium hydroxide aqueous solution (5.0 M) to a pH value of 9 and then freeze dried to produce the functionalized CQDs with different amounts of amino groups. They are referred to as CQD-EA/CA-0 (control), CQD-EA/CA-0.1 and CQD- EA/CA -0.2, where the numbers 0, 0.1 and 0.2 stand for the mass ratios of ethanolamine to citric acid during the hydrothermal reaction, respectively. The synthesized CQDs were characterized with Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Vertex 70, Bruker, USA) with a wavenumber range of 4000-500 cm-1 and an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Kratos AXIS UltraDLD spectrometer, Kratos Analytical Ltd) with a mono Al Kα X-ray source to investigate the changes of chemical composition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the hollow fiber substrates of TFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The substrates of the TFC membranes were fabricated by a co-extrusion technique via a dry-jet wet spinning process with a dual layer spinneret. Firstly, the vacuum-dried PES polymer was dissolved in a mixture of NMP and PEG (1:1) at 60°C. Then a small portion of DI water was added into the fully dissolved PES polymer solution dropwise. Subsequently, the DI water, homogenous PES polymer solution and NMP were extruded from a dual layer spinneret via the inner, middle and outer channels, respectively, to produce the hollow fiber substrates with a dense layer at the lumen side. More details about the hollow fiber spinning process could be found elsewhere [42,43]. Then the as-spun membranes were post-treated with tap water for 2 days and a mixture of DI water and glycerol (1:1) for another 2 days before being air dried in room temperature for 2 days. At last, three pieces of the PES hollow fiber substrates were made into a small lab-scale module with a length of about 14 cm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication, modification and post-treatment of the polyamide layers of TFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The polyamide layers of the TFC membranes were fabricated on the inner surface of the hollow fiber substrates via conventional interfacial polymerization reaction. Firstly, a 2 wt% MPD aqueous solution with 0.1 wt% SDS was firstly pumped through the lumen side of the hollow fiber substrates for 3 min at a flow rate of 4.25 ml/min, then compressed air was applied through the lumen side of the hollow fiber substrates for 5 min to get rid of the excessive MPD solution. Then a TMC solution, which was dissolved in hexane at 0.15 wt%, was pumped through the lumen side of the hollow fiber substrates for 5 min at a flow rate of 2.50 ml/min to engage the reaction with the MPD aqueous solution remained on the inner surface. Afterwards, compressed air was applied through the lumen side of the hollow fiber substrates for 1 min to remove the excessive TMC solution. To modify the polyamide layers, one of the synthesized CQD-EA/CA-0, CQD-EA/CA-0.1 or CQD-EA/CA-0.2 was added into the MPD aqueous solution at 1 wt%. The resultant TFC membranes were referred to as TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0)-1, TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 and TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.2)-1, respectively, where 1 stands for the concentration of the synthesized CQDs in the MPD aqueous solutions. The small lab-scale modules after interfacial polymerization were left in air overnight and then immersed in DI water for one day before being tested or post-treated. Then the modified TFC membrane, which had the optimal separation performance, was treated with a sodium hypochlorite aqueous solution at 2000 ppm, 4000 ppm and 8000 ppm for 3h, respectively, to investigate the effects of hypochlorite treatment on separation performance and physicochemical properties of the newly developed TFC membranes incorporated with amino functionalized CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of the polyamide layers of TFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para: A variety of characterization techniques were employed to investigate the physicochemical changes of the polyamide layers after modifications (i.e., incorporation of amino functionalized CQDs and hypochlorite treatment). For sample preparation, the TFC hollow fiber membranes were rinsed with DI water completely to eliminate the potential contaminants before being freeze dried. Field emission scanning electronic microscopy (FESEM, JEOL JSM-6700) was utilized to examine the morphology of the unmodified and modified polyamide layers, for which the TFC membranes were fractured in liquid nitrogen and coated with platinum using a JOEL JFC-1100E ion sputtering device. Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Nanoscope IIIa, Digital Instrument, USA) was used to determine surface topography (i.e., mean roughness (Ra), root mean square roughness (Rq) and effective surface area) of the unmodified and modified polyamide layers with a scan size of 5 μm × 5 μm under a tapping mode at room temperature in air. An X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Kratos AXIS UltraDLD spectrometer, Kratos Analytical Ltd) with a mono Al Kα X-ray source was utilized to study the chemical composition changes of the modified polyamide layers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance of the TFC membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The separation performance of the unmodified and modified TFC membranes was examined in terms of pure water permeability (PWP or A, L m-2 h-1/bar, LMH/bar) and solute rejection (R, %) using small lab-scale modules with an effective membrane area of about 7.4–7.8 cm2. Prior to each test, all the TFC membranes were stabilized at 20 bar for 1h. The stabilization could not only increase the membrane surface area, but also stretch the polyamide selective layer (i.e., decrease water transport length and resistance) as well as lower the substrate tortuosity [44]. To evaluate the PWP of the TFC membranes, DI water with a volume of 4.5 L was circulated through the lumen side of the small lab-scale modules at 15 bar with a flow rate of 200 ml/min. The PWP was calculated with the equation below:
(1)
A =
∆ V
A m
∆ t ∆ P
where ∆ V
L
is the volume of the water permeate collected from the shell side of the small lab-scale modules during a time interval ∆ t
h
under a trans-membrane pressure of ∆ P
b a r
, and
A m
(
m 2
)
is the effective membrane area of the small lab-scale modules.

To evaluate the salt rejection of the TFC membranes, a 2000 ppm NaCl aqueous solution with a volume of 4.5 L was circulated through the lumen side of the small lab-scale modules at 15 bar with a flow rate of 200 ml/min. The conductivity of the feed solution and permeate collected from the shell side of the small lab-scale modules was measured to calculate the salt rejection of the TFC membranes using the equations below:
(2)
R =
(
1 −
C p
C f
)
× 100 %
where
C p
and
C f
are the salt concentrations (which were determined by a conductivity meter) of the permeate and feed solutions, respectively.

Fig. 1 compares the FT-IR spectra of surface chemistry on CQDs with and without amino functionalization. For all the synthesized CQDs, a broad absorption of O–H stretching around 3300 cm−1 and an absorption of CO stretching at 1567 cm−1 are observed, indicating the existence of carboxyl groups in these CQDs [45,46]. Different from the control (i.e., CQD-EA/CA-0), two characteristic peaks at 1196 cm−1 and 1723 cm−1 are found in the spectra of the amino functionalized CQDs (i.e., CQD-EA/CA-0.1 and CQD-EA/CA-0.2), which could be attributed to the C–N stretching and CO stretching of the aliphatic ester, respectively [47]. These confirm that CQD-EA/CA-0.1 and CQD-EA/CA-0.2 are successully functionalized with amino groups. The surface chemistry of the synthesized CQDs was further quantitatively investigated by XPS, and the results are summarized in Table 1 . All synthesized CQDs have a high oxygen content of more than 30%, which indicates many hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups existing on these CQDs. Since all the synthesized CQDs were neutralized with sodium hydroxide, there also exists around 10–15% Na content in these CQDs. The existence of oxygen-containing groups and sodium could impart the synthesized CQDs with excellent hydrophilicity. Besides, a negligible N content could be detected in the control CQDs (i.e., CQD-EA/CA-0), while the N contents in CQD-EA/CA-0.1 and CQD-EA/CA-0.2 are 2.55% and 3.19%, respectively, due to the increased ratio of EA to CA during the hydrothermal reaction. In summary, CQD-EA/CA-0.1 and CQD-EA/CA-0.2 have been successfully synthesized with amino functionality, which could react with TMC and play a more active role in the subsequent interfacial polymerization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of the polyamide layers incorporated with CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: FESEM was empolyed to characterize the cross-section and surface morphology of the polyamide layers incorporated with CQD-EA/CA-0, CQD-EA/CA-0.1 and CQD-EA/CA-0.2 in comparison with the unmodified polyamide layer, as presented in Fig. 2 . All the TFC membranes have a visible and independent polyamide layer on top of the inner surface of the PES hollow fiber substrates. Fig. 2 (a1 and b1) show that the unmodified TFC membrane has a relatively smooth polyamide layer with a nodular-like surface morphology. In contrast, the TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0)-1, TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 and TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.2)-1 membranes have rougher polyamide layers showing the typical ridge-and-valley surface morphology with more and broader leaves than the unmodified one. The morphological change may result from the presence of CQDs during the interfacial polymerization where they interfere the crosslinking reaction between the two monomers and create more diffusion channels in the nascent polyamide network. As a result, more MPD molecules in the aqueous phase could diffuse across the nascent polyamide layer and react with TMC molecules in the organic phase until the polyamide layer becomes dense and thick enough to stop the MPD diffusion and terminate the crosslinking reaction.

The surface topology of the polyamide layers incorporated with CQD-EA/CA-0, CQD-EA/CA-0.1 and CQD-EA/CA-0.2 was probed by AFM. Fig. 3 presents the AFM images and the measured Rq, Ra and effective surface areas. All the modified TFC membranes have a polyamide layer with higher roughness than the unmodified one. Consistent with the order of N content in the synthesized CQDs (Table 1), the Rq and Ra values follow the order of TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.2)-1 > TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 > TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0)-1. Clearly, the increasing amino content in the functionalized CQDs may facilitate their participation with the interfacial polymerization reaction. The effective surface areas of TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0)-1, TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 and TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.2)-1 are larger than that of the unmodified one (i.e., 35–36 vs. 31 μm2). It is widely believed that TFC membranes with higher roughness would also have a higher water permeability because the higher roughness results in the larger effective surface area for water transport across the membranes [48,49].

Table 2 summarizes the XPS results and reveals the effects of incorporating these CQDs on the chemical compositions of polyamide layers. Although all the three kinds of CQDs have a Na content of 10–15%, only 1 wt% of the CQDs were added into the MPD aqueous solutions. Therefore, there only exists a negligible Na content in the modified polyamide layers. All the modified polyamide layers have a slightly higher O content than the unmodified polyamide layer because the incorporated CQDs have high oxygen content, as shown in Table 1. The higher O content in the modified polyamide layers may potentially improve their water permeability. Among the modified TFC membranes, the polyamide layer of TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 shows the highest oxygen content of 16.22%. In contrast, the polyamide layer of TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.2)-1 has a lower oxygen content of 16.09% because this kind of CQDs (i.e., CQD-EA/CA-0.2) has a higher N content of 3.19% and a lower oxygen content of 32.83% (Table 1). Consistent with the order of N content on CQDs (Table 1), the N content in the polyamide layers follows the same trend as follow: the unmodified TFC membrane < TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0)-1 < TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 < TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.2)-1. Clearly, when the amino functionalized CQDs react with TMC, it would bring more functionalized CQDs into the interfacial polymerization and result in a higher oxygen and nitrogen content in the polyamide layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance of the TFC membranes incorporated with CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 compares the PWP and salt rejection of the TFC membranes with and without the synthesized CQDs. The unmodified TFC membrane has a PWP of 3.87 LMH/bar and a NaCl rejection of 98.6% against a 2000 ppm NaCl aqueous solution at 15 bar. Comparing with the unmodified TFC membrane, TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0)-1, TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 and TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.2)-1 have 17.8%, 42.1% and 8.8% higher PWP values, respectively, but with slightly lower NaCl rejections.

Generally, the enhanced PWPs of the modified TFC membranes could be attributed to the physicochemical changes of the polyamide layers induced by the incorporated CQDs, such as higher roughness and effective surface area, and the existence of more hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups in the polyamide network. However, since the synthesized CQDs have slightly different chemical compositions from one another, the resultant polyamide layers also show slightly different performance. Among them, TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 has the highest PWP of 5.50 LMH/bar at 15 bar. TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 has a higher PWP than TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0)-1 because the former has amino functionalized CQDs that could react with TMC and bring more CQDs into the polyamide layer, thus resulting in more performance enhancement than the latter ones. The lower PWP of TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.2)-1 than TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 is due to the fact that the former has less hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups on the polyamide layer than the latter as indicated by its lower oxygen content (i.e., 16.09 vs. 16.22%) as shown in Table 2. Therefore, one must balance the content of N and O elements in the synthesized CQDs in the future work in order to maximize their effects.

It should be noted that the NaCl rejections of all the modified TFC membranes are still ≥98%. The slightly lower NaCl rejections of the modified TFC membranes may be caused by the interference of CQDs on the crosslinking reaction between MPD and TMC monomers. However, the variation in NaCl rejection among all membranes is very small. This implies that the incorporation of 1 wt% synthesized CQDs into the interfacial polymerization does not induce significant defects to the polyamide layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of the polyamide layer of TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 after hypochlorite treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Since the TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 membrane has the highest PWP with a comparable salt rejection, it was chosen for the study of hypochlorite treatment. Its polyamide layer was treated with a sodium hypochlorite aqueous solution at 2000 ppm, 4000 ppm and 8000 ppm for 3h, respectively. Fig. 5 (a2-a4) and (b2-b4) show the cross-section and surface morphology of the polyamide layers after hypochlorite treatment. Compared with the one without any post-treatment (Fig. 5 (a1) and (b1)), the sodium hypochlorite treated polyamide layers still have the similar typical ridge-and-valley surface morphology. Therefore, there are no obvious changes observed for the polyamide layer of TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 after hypochlorite treatment. The AFM results presented in Fig. 6 also confirm our observation by FESEM. All the polyamide layers with or without hypochlorite treatment have the similar Rq, Ra and effective surface area.

Table 3 shows the XPS results reflecting the effects of hypochlorite treatment on the chemical compositions of the polyamide layer of TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1. Generally, the measured Cl content shows an increasing trend with an increase in sodium hypochlorite concentration. The Cl content in the polyamide layer of TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 increases to 2.68%, 4.53% and 7.34% after being treated with sodium hypochlorite aqueous solutions at 2000 ppm, 4000 ppm and 8000 ppm for 3h, respectively. The chlorine is likely bonded to the polyamide layer by replacing the hydrogen on N–H groups, weakening the hydrogen bonds among the polyamide network. This replacement would provide more rotational freedom and flexibility for the polymer chains [37]. Alternatively, the chlorine might be bonded to the ring structure undermining the amide bonds and breaking the polyamide chains, which would result in a less cross-linked polyamide layer [39,41]. Both chlorination mechanisms would lead to a higher water permeability across the TFC membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance of TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 after hypochlorite treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows the effects of hypochlorite treatment on PWP and salt rejection of the TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 membrane. After hypochlorite treatment, the PWP value is increased significantly. Comparing with the one without treatment, PWP jumps about 54.2%, 84.2% and 218.7% after the membrane is treated with a sodium hypochlorite aqueous solution at 2000 ppm, 4000 ppm and 8000 ppm for 3h, respectively. The increasing PWP trend is consistent with the increasing Cl content observed in Table 3 for the hypochlorite treated polyamide layers. As discussed in the previous section, the chlorine bonded to the polyamide layer would either weaken the hydrogen bonding among the polyamide network or undermine the amide bonds of the polymer chains, resulting in a less cross-linked polyamide layer.

In terms of salt rejection, the TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 membrane without any treatment has a NaCl rejection of 98.0% against a 2000 ppm NaCl feed solution at 15 bar. After being treated with 2000 ppm and 4000 ppm sodium hypochlorite for 3h, the NaCl rejection of the membranes jumps to 98.8% and 98.9%, respectively. This surprising phenomenon may result from the enhanced charge repulsion effect against charge solutes as a result of the greater Cl content on the polyamide layer despite of the reduced cross-linking degree [39,40]. Nevertheless, when the sodium hypochlorite concentration is further increased to 8000 ppm, the NaCl rejection of the treated membrane drops to 97.2%. This could be explained by the highest Cl content of 7.34% observed on the treated polyamide layer, as shown in Table 3. Since the chlorine bonded to the polyamide layer would result in a less cross-linked polyamide layer, the highest Cl content implies the least cross-linked polyamide layer. The aforementioned two mechanisms are competing with each other, having different effects on salt rejection. When the negative effects from the breaking of the polyamide chains with a least cross-linked polyamide layer on NaCl rejection cannot be compensated by the enhanced charge repulsion effects, the NaCl rejection of the TFC membrane starts to decline.

Since a moderate hypochlorite treatment could significantly enhance the PWP and slightly improve the NaCl rejection of the TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 membrane with an impressive PWP at 10.13 LMH/bar and a NaCl rejection of 98.9%, it could be employed as an effective post-treatment method for our newly developed TFC membrane. Table 4 shows a benchmarking between our membrane and those BWRO membranes consisting of nanomaterials reported in the literature. Our newly developed TFC membrane after hypochlorite treatment has superior separation performance to the state-of-the-art BWRO membranes. Thus, it should have a great potential for brackish water desalination.

Two different approaches, i.e., incorporation of amino functionalized CQDs and hypochlorite treatment, have been employed in this work to modify the polyamide layer of TFC hollow fiber membranes with significantly enhanced separation performance for BWRO. The following conclusions can be drawn:
(1)
The amino functionalized CQDs can participate with the cross-linking reaction during the interfacial polymerization and result in a novel polyamide layer with improved physicochemical properties and separation performance.
(2)
After incorporation of 1 wt% CQD-EA/CA-0.1 into the polyamide layer, the resultant TFC membranes has a 42.1% jump in PWP from 3.87 LMH/bar to 5.50 LMH/bar, while its NaCl rejection remains at 98.0% using a simulated brackish water as the feed at 15 bar. The enhanced performance arises from the morphological and composition changes of the polyamide layer, such as higher roughness, larger effective surface area and the existence of more hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups.
(3)
After being treated with a sodium hypochlorite aqueous solution at 4000 ppm for 3h, the PWP of the TFC-(CQD-EA/CA-0.1)-1 membrane is further promoted to 10.13 LMH/bar under the same testing conditions. Meanwhile, its NaCl rejection is also improved slightly from 98.0% to 98.9%. The performance enhancement may result from a less cross-linked polyamide layer and a higher charge repulsion effect against charged solutes after the hypochlorite treatment.
(4)
Future search should focus on the balance of N and O content in the synthesized CQDs in the future work in order to maximize their effects.

Wenxiao Gai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing-Original Draft, Writing-Review & Editing, Yu Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing-Original Draft, Qipeng Zhao: Conceptualization, Writing-Original Draft, Tai-Shung Chung *: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing-Original Draft, Writing-Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research work was funded by PUB, Singapore's National Water Agency under the project entitled “Molecular design of thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes for brackish water and seawater desalination” and NUS grant No. of R-279-000-583-490. The authors also wish to sincerely thank Dr. Kangjia Lu for her very kind help and training on the FESEM operation during the Covid-19 special period and Dr. Susilo Japip and Dr. Dieling Zhao for their valuable advices and kind support.
